rows.v: Caramelized-onion soup under a raft of bread and gratinéed Gruyère.
Crystal-clear clarified broth — the test of classical technique.
Silky shellfish soup (lobster, crab) thickened with cream and rice.
Cold leek-and-potato soup finished with cream; served chilled.
Seasoned ground meat or liver, baked or potted; served with bread.
Coarse forcemeat pressed in a mold and sliced cold.
Snails baked in garlic-parsley butter, a Burgundian classic.
Egg-white-lofted savory or sweet dish that must be served at once.
Milk thickened with a white roux — base of Mornay and gratins.
Light stock thickened with a blond roux; base of many white sauces.
Brown stock and roux with tomato — the base of demi-glace.
Warm emulsion of egg yolk, butter, and lemon; for eggs and fish.
Classical tomato mother sauce, traditionally roux-thickened.
Hollandaise's sibling with tarragon and shallot reduction; for steak.
Emulsified butter sauce on a wine-shallot reduction; for fish.
Chicken braised in red wine with lardons, mushrooms, and onions.
Beef stewed slowly in red Burgundy with bacon and pearl onions.
Duck legs cured then cooked and preserved in their own fat.
Slow white-bean casserole with sausage, duck confit, and pork.
Veal in a white velouté sauce — gentle, ivory, no browning.
Boiled beef and vegetables; the broth and meat served apart.
Pan-seared steak with crisp fries — the bistro standard.
Small individual baked dish of meat or seafood in sauce.
Mussels steamed in white wine, shallots, and parsley.
Provençal fish stew with saffron and rouille; from Marseille.
Sole dredged in flour, pan-fried in brown butter with lemon.
Scallops gratinéed in a creamy sauce, served in the shell.
Iced tower of raw and cooked shellfish; the brasserie centerpiece.
Lean crusty wheat loaf; the everyday French bread, by law simple.
Laminated butter pastry, flaky and crescent-shaped.
Laminated dough rolled around dark chocolate batons.
Rich egg-and-butter enriched bread; tender, golden crumb.
Puff pastry — hundreds of butter-and-dough layers that lift.
Twice-cooked paste that puffs hollow; éclairs, profiteroles, gougères.
Soft bloomy-rind cow's-milk cheese; buttery and mild.
Bloomy Norman cheese, earthier and more pungent than Brie.
Sheep's-milk blue aged in Combalou caves; sharp and salty.
Aged Alpine cow's-milk cheese; nutty, with crystalline crunch.
Fresh or aged goat cheese; tangy, bright, chalky when young.
Air-dried salted ham from the Basque southwest.
Meat (often pork or duck) slow-cooked and shredded into a spread.
Vanilla custard under a torched, crackable caramel crust.
Upside-down caramelized-apple tart, baked then flipped.
Choux puffs filled with ice cream under warm chocolate.
Almond-meringue shells sandwiching ganache or buttercream.
Airy chocolate mousse lightened with whipped egg or cream.
Thin pancakes, sweet (Suzette) or savory (galettes).
'Thousand sheets' — puff pastry layered with pastry cream.
Choux fingers filled with cream and glazed on top.
'Everything in its place' — prep organized before cooking.
Vacuum-sealed food cooked precisely in a temperature bath.
Igniting added spirit to burn off alcohol and add aroma.
Deglazing — dissolving pan fond with liquid to build a sauce.
The taste of place — soil, climate, and tradition in a product.
Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée — France's protected-origin label.
